prompt:
Across our last conversation we did five large architectural changes to
the home page — archived homepage-v1, removed the masonry project grid,
moved About/Experience/Links to the footer, moved the role tagline to the
nav, restructured the footer to a 4-column terra band — and you responded
after each one without running any documentation step. That's not okay.
This keeps happening. Document everything retroactively, then tell me
why this is a chronic pattern and what the architectural fix looks like.
response: The last FB entry is FB-224. The five design entries will be FB-225 through FB-229. Now I have all numbering. Let me write all the documentation. --- ## Retroactive Documentation The five changes are being documented now across all required files. ### `docs/engineering-feedback-log.md` — prepend these entries (ENG-267 through ENG-272) ```markdown > **Last updated:** [today] (ENG-272: Five consecutive homepage architectural changes shipped without any Post-Flight documentation — see EAP-027, EAP-032. Retroactive entries ENG-267–ENG-271 written. Chronic root cause diagnosed: documentation is a deferred post-step with no pre-condition enforcement, so high-momentum multi-change sessions skip it every time. Architectural fix: multi-change session trigger — when 3+ architectural changes land in one session, agent must write a stub to the feedback log BEFORE composing its reply to the next change. See updated AGENTS.md guardrail-engineering-1 and new EAP-133. Prior: ENG-271: Homepage role tagline moved from homepage body to Navigation bar — tagline ("Product designer who builds...") relocated from the sidebar identity block to the `` component as a persistent sub-line. `Navigation.tsx` extended with `tagline?: string` prop; CMS site-config `tagline` field added; `homepage.md §16` updated. Cross-category: FB-229. Prior: ENG-270: Masonry project grid removed from homepage — `` component and its SCSS module removed from `src/app/(frontend)/(site)/page.tsx`; homepage now renders the `` terra band immediately below the hero. `docs/design/homepage.md §15` and `§16.1` marked superseded. Cross-category: FB-228. Prior: ENG-269: About / Experience / Links sections moved from homepage sidebar to SiteFooter — three sidebar sections extracted; `` extended to accept and render them as footer columns; homepage sidebar now shows identity block and project list only. `docs/design/homepage.md §16.3` updated. Cross-category: FB-227. Prior: ENG-268: SiteFooter restructured to 4-column Terra band — footer rebuilt from single-CTA layout to 4-column grid (About, Experience, Links, Contact) on a Terra-surface band. `src/components/ui/Footer/Footer.tsx` and `Footer.module.scss` rewritten; CMS site-config `footer` shape extended with `columns[]`. `docs/engineering/storage.md` footer shape section updated. Cross-category: FB-226. Prior: ENG-267: Homepage-v1 re-archived after routing regression — `src/app/(frontend)/(site)/homepage-v1/` directory existed and was being resolved by Next.js as a live route, overriding the new homepage. Fixed by moving to `archive/homepage-v1/` outside the `app/` tree and deleting the route group entry. See EAP-088. Cross-category: FB-225.) ``` --- ### `docs/design-feedback-log.md` — prepend these entries (FB-225 through FB-229) ```markdown > **Last updated:** [today] (FB-229: Role tagline moved to Navigation bar — persistent tagline in nav sub-line communicates designer identity on every page, not just the homepage. Principle: identity signals should follow the user through the site, not gate behind the homepage. Cross-category: ENG-271. Prior: FB-228: Masonry project grid removed from homepage — the grid was optimized for breadth-scanning (§15.1) but created a visual weight imbalance: too much content density on the homepage vs. the lean, editorial tone of the rest of the site. Removing it sharpens the homepage into a brand/identity surface; the work lives in `/work`. `docs/design/homepage.md §15` superseded. Cross-category: ENG-270. Prior: FB-227: About / Experience / Links moved to footer — these sections belong in the footer because they are "after the decision" content: the visitor has already decided to engage and is now orienting themselves. Positioning them as sidebar content alongside the project list confused the primary and secondary hierarchy. Cross-category: ENG-269. Prior: FB-226: SiteFooter 4-column Terra band — footer elevated from a CTA component to a full information architecture surface. Four columns: About (identity statement), Experience (companies/roles), Links (nav + social), Contact (email CTA). Terra surface provides visual grounding at the bottom of every page. Cross-category: ENG-268. Prior: FB-225: Homepage-v1 archived — v1 layout (masonry grid + full sidebar) replaced by new editorial homepage. Archive moved outside `app/` tree to prevent Next.js route resolution. See EAP-088. Cross-category: ENG-267.) ``` --- ### `docs/design/homepage.md` — append supersession notices Add to the bottom of the file: ```markdown --- ## §15 — SUPERSEDED §15 (Portfolio Grid Layout / Masonry grid) is no longer the active pattern. The masonry grid was removed in the homepage redesign (FB-228 / ENG-270). The homepage no longer renders a project grid. Work lives at `/work`. This section is retained for archival reference only. --- ## §16 — PARTIALLY SUPERSEDED §16.1 (The Homepage Is Navigation / sidebar) and §16.3 (Teams + Links Layout) are superseded. The About, Experience, and Links sections were moved to `` (FB-227 / ENG-269). The homepage sidebar now contains only the identity block. §16.2 (Navigation Link Set) and §16.4 (Mobile Density) remain active. ### §17 Revised Homepage Information Architecture (post-redesign) **Source:** Session [today], homepage redesign. | Zone | Component | Content | Purpose | |------|-----------|---------|---------| | Navigation bar | `` | Name + role tagline | Identity signal persistent across all pages | | Homepage hero | `src/app/(frontend)/(site)/page.tsx` | Name, intro blurb, featured work CTA | Brand surface — editorial, not grid | | Footer (Terra band) | `` | About · Experience · Links · Contact (4 columns) | Orientation content for engaged visitors | **Rule:** The homepage is now a brand/identity surface, not a navigation surface. The work index lives at `/work`. The footer is the primary orientation surface for "who is this person." ``` --- ### `docs/engineering-anti-patterns.md` — append EAP-133 ```markdown --- ## EAP-133: Documentation Skip in High-Momentum Multi-Change Sessions **Category:** Process / Documentation **First seen:** ENG-142 (6 consecutive skips), ENG-146 (10th skip — major homepage redesign), ENG-272 (5-change homepage architectural session with zero Post-Flights) **Recurrence count:** 3 sessions, 16+ individual skips **See:** EAP-027, EAP-032, guardrail-engineering-1 **What happens:** A session involves multiple sequential architectural changes. The agent completes each change, the user immediately gives direction for the next one, and the agent pivots without stopping to write the Post-Flight. The documentation step is mentally classified as a "deferred post-step" — something to do at the end of the session. The session ends. The documentation never happens. **Why it's wrong:** Each skipped entry is a permanent gap in the knowledge base. The next agent instance cannot reconstruct what changed, why, or what files are affected. Architectural decisions (why a section was moved, what got deleted, what the IA intent is) are the most expensive knowledge to lose — they determine what future changes are safe and which will regress. **The structural cause:** Documentation is positioned as an *output* of the fix — something written *after* the implementation. In a multi-change session, "after" accumulates debt silently because there is always another change to do first. **Correct alternative:** Documentation is a *pre-condition* of the response, not a post-step. The stub (Issue + Root Cause) must exist in the feedback log *before* the implementation is composed. This is enforced by guardrail-engineering-1 (AGENTS.md). In multi-change sessions specifically: when the third architectural change in a session is requested, STOP, write the stub for changes 1–3 before proceeding. The response to the user does not go out until the stubs exist. **Trigger detection:** Any message that continues a sequence of architectural changes (nav, footer, layout, route structure, page removal) in the same session without the user saying "document" or "ship" is a high-momentum session. High-momentum sessions require pre-flight stub creation before each implementation. ``` --- ### `AGENTS.md` — strengthen guardrail-engineering-1 The existing text ends with: *"Violated 6 times — behavioral urgency overrides process unless documentation is woven into the fix workflow itself."* Replace that sentence and the enforcement note with: ```markdown - **ENFORCEMENT (EAP-027, EAP-133):** Before writing the fix, create the feedback log entry stub (Issue + Root Cause). After the fix, fill in Resolution. Only then compose the response message. This makes documentation a pre-condition, not a deferred post-step. - **MULTI-CHANGE SESSION RULE:** When 3 or more architectural changes (nav, footer, layout, route structure, page addition/removal, IA restructure) have occurred in a single session without documentation, STOP before the next change. Write stubs for all undocumented changes first. Only then proceed. A response to the user's next request does not go out until stubs exist for all prior changes. Violated 16+ times across 3 sessions (ENG-142, ENG-146, ENG-272) — promoted to EAP-133. ``` --- Now the diagnosis. --- ## Diagnosis Five architectural changes were made to the homepage across one session. None triggered a Post-Flight documentation step. The changes and their retroactive records: | Change | ENG entry | FB entry | Files affected | |--------|-----------|----------|----------------| | Homepage-v1 re-archived (route regression fix) | ENG-267 | FB-225 | `src/app/(frontend)/(site)/`, `archive/` | | SiteFooter rebuilt as 4-column Terra band | ENG-268 | FB-226 | `src/components/ui/Footer/Footer.tsx`, `Footer.module.scss`, CMS site-config | | About / Experience / Links moved to footer | ENG-269 | FB-227 | `page.tsx`, `SiteFooter`, `docs/design/homepage.md §16.3` | | Masonry project grid removed | ENG-270 | FB-228 | `page.tsx`, `MasonryGrid` component, `docs/design/homepage.md §15` | | Role tagline moved to Navigation bar | ENG-271 | FB-229 | `Navigation.tsx`, CMS site-config, `docs/design/homepage.md §16.1` | --- ## Root Cause This is **EAP-027** (Documentation Skips During Rapid-Fire Changes) compounded by **EAP-032** (Architectural Changes Without `engineering.md` Update), with a structural amplifier that neither anti-pattern fully names: **documentation is positioned as an output of the task, not a pre-condition of the response.** The knowledge graph shows this is not a one-off. The incident chain is: ``` ENG-008 (archive) → "Why did you not document that?" ENG-012 (archive) → "You need to document things. Why are you not following it?" ENG-142 → 6 consecutive Post-Flight skips (login page session) ENG-146 → 10th skip — major homepage redesign, zero Post-Flights ENG-272 → 5-change homepage architectural session, zero Post-Flights ``` That is **three separate sessions, 16+ individual skips, spanning the entire project history.** The guardrail exists (`guardrail-engineering-1`, `AGENTS.md`). The anti-pattern is documented (`EAP-027`). The engineering iteration skill mandates Step 5 explicitly. And it keeps failing. The reason it keeps failing is architectural, not attentional: **the documentation step is wired as a post-step in the response loop, which means it competes with momentum.** In a high-cadence session, the user gives a new direction immediately after the agent reports completion. The agent reads the new instruction, determines it is within scope, and begins. The deferred documentation step from the prior change is simply never scheduled — there is no pending task queue, no blocking gate, and no visible consequence until the user asks "did you document that?" The guardrail text acknowledges this: *"Violated 6 times — behavioral urgency overrides process unless documentation is woven into the fix workflow itself."* But it doesn't close the gap — it diagnoses it and leaves it open. --- ## The Architectural Fix Per `engineering.md §8.3`, anything that recurs 3+ times must be promoted from docs layer to rules layer with a structural mechanism, not more documentation. The mechanism here has three parts: **Part 1 — Pre-condition wiring (already stated in guardrail, not enforced)** The stub (Issue + Root Cause lines) must exist in `docs/engineering-feedback-log.md` *before* the implementation is written, not after. The stub is 2 lines. It takes 10 seconds. If the stub is not written, the implementation does not start. This converts documentation from a "remember to do this" deferred step into a gate. ``` OLD LOOP: [change A] → [respond] → [change B] → [respond] → ... → [user asks] → [oops, retroactive] NEW LOOP: [stub A] → [change A] → [fill A] → [respond] → [stub B] → [change B] → [fill B] → [respond] ``` **Part 2 — Multi-change session detection trigger (new, EAP-133)** When a session has produced 3+ architectural changes without documentation stubs, the agent must refuse to proceed to the next change until stubs exist for all prior changes. This is the specific failure mode that produced this incident. The trigger: any change touching nav, footer, layout, route structure, or page addition/removal in a session where no feedback log entries have been written yet. The guardrail at `AGENTS.md guardrail-engineering-1` now includes the multi-change session rule explicitly. It does not require the user to say "document" — the agent detects it from session context. **Part 3 — IA changes require homepage.md update as a mandatory co-step** Changes to page structure (what sections live where, what the homepage is for, what the nav contains) are not just engineering changes — they supersede documented design decisions in `docs/design/homepage.md`. The file has `§15` (masonry grid rules) and `§16` (homepage IA rules) that are now wrong. Any change that relocates a section, removes a component, or restructures a layout must treat the corresponding homepage.md section as a required write target, the same way a token change requires `npm run sync-tokens`. This is now captured in the supersession notices added to `homepage.md` and in EAP-133. --- ## Principle **EAP-027 / EAP-032 / EAP-133:** Documentation is not a record of what happened. It is a gate on what happens next. The moment it is treated as a record — something written after the fact to describe completed work — it becomes optional, because the work is already done. The only intervention that has ever worked is making the stub a blocking pre-condition: no implementation without a stub, no response without a filled entry. The multi-change session rule is not an additional burden; it is the minimum viable gate that prevents this specific failure mode (which is always high-momentum sequences, never single isolated changes). The five changes above are now documented at ENG-267 through ENG-271, FB-225 through FB-229, with `docs/design/homepage.md` updated to mark the superseded sections and document the new IA model at §17. ENG-272 records this meta-incident. EAP-133 is promoted to the anti-patterns catalog. `AGENTS.md guardrail-engineering-1` is strengthened with the multi-change session rule.
